prompt: Adapt the following Grade 6 lesson plan on fractions for a multilingual classroom where 60% of students are English language learners. Preserve learning objectives. Original plan: [30-min lesson, textbook problems, whole-class instruction on equivalent fractions].
reference: Adaptations: visual fraction models, bilingual key-vocabulary cards, partner discussion in home language, manipulatives (fraction strips), reduced reading load, sentence frames for sharing answers.

prompt: A lesson on photosynthesis was designed for a well-resourced urban school with a lab. Adapt it for a rural school with no lab equipment, limited textbooks, and unreliable electricity. Grade 8 biology.
reference: Replace lab with outdoor observations of local plants. Use hand-drawn diagrams instead of projector. Sun-powered demonstration. Peer teaching of key terms. Local plant examples instead of imported textbook examples.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 10 history lesson on World War I (originally designed for European students) for learners in East Africa. Adjust context and examples while maintaining the curriculum objectives.
reference: Include the African theatre of WWI, impact on colonial subjects, local examples of conscription and resource extraction. Maintain analysis skills objectives. Reduce Eurocentric assumptions in discussion prompts.

prompt: A Grade 5 mathematics lesson on multiplication was planned for 60-minute periods. Adapt it for 30-minute periods without losing core concepts.
reference: Prioritise: single core concept per period. Remove extension activities. Use exit ticket to check understanding quickly. Defer word problems to next period. Homework bridges the gap.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 9 English literature lesson on Shakespeare for students with low prior reading ability. The lesson objective is to analyse character motivation in Macbeth Act 1.
reference: Use graphic novel version or illustrated summary. Pre-teach key vocabulary with visual glossary. Provide sentence frames for analysis. Use film clip (2 minutes) before text. Scaffold written response with structured frame.

prompt: A Grade 7 science lesson on the rock cycle was designed for a country with igneous rock formations nearby. Adapt it for a country with predominantly sedimentary landscapes and no volcanic activity.
reference: Replace volcanic rock focus with sedimentary formation examples from local landscape. Use river and coastal erosion as local entry point. Adjust fieldwork to local geology. Keep full rock cycle framework.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 8 ICT lesson on spreadsheets (originally requires individual computers) for a classroom with only two computers shared among 35 students.
reference: Rotation model: 4-5 students per computer in timed slots. Others do paper-based spreadsheet exercises. Teacher demonstration on one screen. Peer-teaching when skilled students finish first. Offline practice worksheets.

prompt: A Grade 11 economics lesson was designed for urban students familiar with banks and formal financial systems. Adapt it for rural students whose primary experience is informal markets and mobile money.
reference: Entry point: mobile money as banking analogy. Use local market examples for supply/demand. Replace stock market references with crop price fluctuations. Keep macroeconomic theory intact but ground in accessible context.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 6 health education lesson on nutrition for a community where the diet is primarily cassava, plantain, and beans. Original lesson uses Western food pyramid.
reference: Replace food pyramid with locally appropriate plate model using available foods. Map local foods to nutritional categories. Discuss local food preparation methods that preserve nutrients. Keep objectives on balanced diet.

prompt: A Grade 10 physics lesson on electricity requires students to handle electrical components. Adapt it for a school with no laboratory materials but students who have mobile phones.
reference: Use phone charging as real-world entry to current, voltage, resistance. Simulation apps if phones available. Paper circuit diagrams for calculations. Video demonstrations for experiments. Conceptual understanding preserved.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 4 reading lesson on comprehension strategies for a class of 55 students with one teacher and no teaching assistant. Original lesson assumes small group rotations.
reference: Whole-class think-aloud for modelling. Peer-pairs for guided practice. Independent application. Circulate using targeted observation checklist. Use 'reading buddies' where strong readers support weaker peers.

prompt: A Grade 9 chemistry lesson on titration requires burettes and lab chemicals. Adapt for a school with no chemicals but access to cooking ingredients (vinegar, baking soda, red cabbage indicator).
reference: Use red cabbage juice as natural pH indicator. Vinegar as acid, baking soda solution as base. Standard syringe instead of burette. Preserve conceptual understanding of neutralisation and endpoint detection.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 7 maths lesson on data representation (charts and graphs) for students who are blind or visually impaired. Maintain the same learning objectives.
reference: Use tactile graphs (raised lines on textured paper or string on cardboard). Verbal description protocols. Braille labels. Audio data sets read aloud. Focus on data interpretation through discussion rather than visual reading.

prompt: A Grade 11 biology lesson on genetics assumes access to a genetics simulation software. Adapt for a school with no computers and limited printed resources.
reference: Card-sort activity simulating genotype combinations. Coin-flip probability experiment to model inheritance ratios. Peer-discussion of pedigree charts on printed hand-outs. Role-play as alleles to model dominant/recessive.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 5 geography lesson on map skills for students with dyscalculia who struggle with scale calculations. Preserve the map reading objectives.
reference: Provide pre-calculated scale reference strips. Use whole-number friendly scales. Focus on directional and feature identification rather than precise calculation. Allow calculator use for distance work.

prompt: A Grade 8 PE lesson on team sports strategy was designed for a gym facility. Adapt it for a classroom or outdoor space with no sports equipment.
reference: Use chairs as markers. Paper-based tactical diagrams. Movement games without balls. Video analysis of tactics. Discussion of strategy principles applicable to any sport.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 10 English lesson on writing persuasive essays for students who are strong oral communicators but weak writers. Same writing objective.
reference: Begin with oral debate to generate ideas. Record speech then transcribe as draft. Use graphic organiser to structure before writing. Allow oral rehearsal of arguments. Scaffold written draft using sentence starters.

prompt: A Grade 6 science lesson on the human digestive system includes a 20-minute video. Adapt for a school with no electricity during school hours.
reference: Replace video with teacher-led diagram building on blackboard. Students draw and label own diagram. Use storytelling: 'follow the sandwich' narrative. Role-play each organ's function. Tactile model using local materials.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 9 maths lesson on probability for students who come from a cultural background where gambling references in examples may be inappropriate.
reference: Replace dice/card gambling examples with: weather forecasting probability, sports match outcomes, seed germination rates, medical test accuracy. Keep all probability concepts and calculations intact.

prompt: A Grade 7 history lesson on ancient Rome assumes students have done background reading at home. Adapt for a context where homework cannot be reliably completed (no electricity, family obligations).
reference: Build all background knowledge in-class. Use a 5-minute prior knowledge activation. Provide brief printed reference card (no reading homework required). Move extended tasks to in-class pair work.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 11 economics lesson on stock markets for students in a country where capital markets are underdeveloped. Preserve the concepts of investment risk and return.
reference: Use land ownership and rental income as investment analogy. Mobile money savings interest rates as return example. Agricultural price risk as market volatility analogue. Maintain conceptual framework with local context.

prompt: A Grade 5 art lesson on perspective drawing requires rulers and specific pencils. Adapt for a classroom with only chalk, blackboards, and minimal stationery.
reference: Demonstrate perspective on blackboard. Students practice on paper using edge of notebook as straight edge. Focus on the concept of vanishing point rather than precision. Local buildings as observational drawing subjects.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 8 chemistry lesson on states of matter for students with hearing impairments in a mainstream class. Teacher uses only verbal instruction currently.
reference: Visual demonstrations with clear sight lines. Written instructions on board. Paired hearing-impaired and hearing student for peer support. Tactile demonstrations (ice melting, steam from hot water). BSL/sign support cards for key terms.

prompt: A Grade 10 English lesson on poetry analysis was written for a British curriculum context. Adapt it for a Caribbean curriculum using Caribbean poets as the primary texts.
reference: Replace British canonical poems with Caribbean poets (e.g., Louise Bennett, Derek Walcott). Keep all poetic device analysis skills. Use dialect poetry as entry point for language variation discussion. Local cultural references.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 6 maths lesson on fractions for students who are significantly behind grade level (operating at Grade 4 level). Same class, differentiated within one lesson.
reference: Three-tier task design: Grade 4 fraction recognition (tier 1), Grade 5 equivalent fractions (tier 2), Grade 6 fraction operations (tier 3). Colour-coded worksheets. Teacher circulates to tier 1 and 2 groups.

prompt: A Grade 9 biology lesson on reproduction includes content that must be taught sensitively. Adapt the lesson for a conservative community where direct discussion of human reproduction is restricted.
reference: Focus on plant and animal reproduction as primary examples. Human reproduction covered factually and clinically using biological terminology. Separate opt-out session available. Parents notified per school policy.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 7 social studies lesson on democracy for a country that does not have a democratic government. Preserve the educational objectives on civic participation.
reference: Frame as comparative civic education: different systems of governance, roles of citizens in each, historical examples. Avoid implying one system is universally superior. Focus on civic responsibility as universal concept.

prompt: A Grade 8 maths lesson on statistics uses datasets about sports. Adapt for a class where girls are disengaged from sports contexts. Same statistical skills objective.
reference: Offer parallel datasets: fashion trends, music streaming, food prices, social media metrics. Students choose their context. Same statistical operations applied. Data interpretation questions are context-free.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 5 English lesson on persuasive writing for students with ADHD. The original is a 60-minute sustained writing task. Same objective.
reference: Break into 10-minute segments with movement breaks. Short modelling burst followed by immediate practice. Choice of topic. Graphic organiser as planning tool. Allow oral dictation as alternative to handwriting.

prompt: A Grade 11 physics lesson on radioactivity requires a Geiger counter demonstration. Adapt for a school with no scientific instruments.
reference: Replace Geiger counter with dice simulation (each die represents an atom, rolled to simulate decay probability). Graphing results mimics decay curve. Conceptual understanding of half-life preserved without equipment.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 6 music lesson on rhythm notation for a school with no musical instruments. Preserve the rhythm reading and performance objectives.
reference: Clapping, tapping, and body percussion replace instruments. Voice percussion (beatboxing basics). Students compose rhythm patterns using syllable notation. Peer performance and evaluation. Same notation reading skills.

prompt: A Grade 9 ICT lesson on creating presentations assumes PowerPoint software. Adapt for schools where students have access only to paper and markers.
reference: Paper-based slide design: each slide on A4 paper. Structure planning worksheet. Oral presentation rehearsal. Peer feedback on clarity and layout. Skills transfer explicitly noted for when digital tools available.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 10 geography lesson on urban planning for students in a rural community with no experience of cities. Preserve the spatial analysis objectives.
reference: Use village layout as entry point for planning concepts. Local land use map drawn collaboratively. Compare to urban photographs. Apply planning principles (zoning, infrastructure) to local context improvement.

prompt: A Grade 7 English lesson requires reading a 500-word text. Adapt it for reluctant readers and students reading below grade level. Same comprehension objectives.
reference: Provide text at two readability levels. Use jigsaw: different student groups read different sections, then teach each other. Key vocabulary pre-taught. Text broken into paragraphs with visual summaries.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 8 chemistry lesson on the periodic table for a class learning in their third language (e.g., French instruction for Swahili-speaking students). Preserve conceptual depth.
reference: Bilingual key-term glossary (French/Swahili). Colour-coded periodic table with labels in both languages. Partner discussion in home language before French output. Visual-heavy slides reduce language load.

prompt: A Grade 5 science lesson on ecosystems includes a fieldwork component. Adapt for a school in a dense urban environment with no green spaces nearby.
reference: Use schoolyard, wall cracks, or potted plants as micro-ecosystems. Urban food chains (pigeons, rats, insects, humans). Street trees and parks mapped from Google Maps printout. Same ecological concepts applied.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 11 maths lesson on calculus for students who have strong conceptual understanding but persistent arithmetic errors. Same calculus objectives.
reference: Allow calculator for arithmetic steps. Focus marking on method, not arithmetic. Error-analysis activities. Calculator-use protocols taught explicitly. Conceptual discussion questions prioritised.

prompt: A Grade 9 history lesson on colonialism was written assuming students have no personal connection to colonial history. Adapt for a class in a formerly colonised country where students' families may have direct historical connections.
reference: Acknowledge local history with sensitivity and respect. Privilege local historical sources and oral histories. Frame discussion to include student heritage, not just coloniser perspective. Trauma-informed facilitation guidance for teacher.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 6 maths lesson on long division for students who have been taught different algorithms in different countries (class has recently arrived refugees using various methods).
reference: Validate all algorithms as correct. Display three methods side-by-side. Students explain their method to a partner. Choose any method to use in assessments. Focus on understanding, not standardising algorithm.

prompt: A Grade 10 biology lesson on disease requires discussing sensitive health topics including HIV/AIDS. Adapt for a community where stigma around HIV is high.
reference: Use clinical, non-stigmatising language throughout. Focus on biology of immune response, not risk behaviour. Teacher professional development note included. Confidential question box allows anonymous questions.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 8 English lesson on formal email writing for students who have no personal email account and limited experience with digital communication. Same objective.
reference: Teach format and conventions using printed examples. Paper-based practice writing formal emails. Discuss when formal vs informal writing is appropriate. Map email conventions to formal letter conventions students already know.

prompt: A Grade 5 social studies lesson on family structures assumes nuclear family as the norm. Adapt for a class with diverse family compositions including single-parent, extended, foster, and same-sex parent families.
reference: Use inclusive language throughout: 'the adults who care for you' instead of 'mum and dad'. Diverse family examples in activities. All family types treated with equal dignity. Discussion grounded in shared values of care and belonging.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 9 physics lesson on waves and sound for an outdoor setting because the school building is unusable due to storm damage. Preserve the learning objectives.
reference: Sound experiments using voice and clapping. Wave demonstrations with a long rope or skipping rope. Echo timing for speed of sound estimation. Students generate and test hypotheses using available outdoor resources.

prompt: A Grade 7 maths lesson on geometry requires protractors and compasses. Adapt for a class with only rulers and pencils available.
reference: Estimate angles by folding paper (right angle, half right angle). Use ruler to construct perpendicular lines. Paper folding for parallel lines. Conceptual understanding of angle properties preserved without instruments.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 11 economics lesson on international trade for students in a small island developing state. Make the global context relevant to their economic reality.
reference: Use fishing exports, tourism, and remittances as local trade examples. Vulnerability to external shocks made central. SIDS-specific trade challenges included. Keep trade theory (comparative advantage, terms of trade) intact.

prompt: A Grade 6 reading lesson relies on a set of graded readers that are not available. Adapt using only free online texts and community-sourced materials.
reference: Use public domain texts (Project Gutenberg), local newspaper excerpts, community notice boards, government leaflets. Pair with comprehension strategy instruction. Teacher-created simplified text at appropriate level.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 8 science lesson on forces for a class that includes three students in wheelchairs. Ensure full participation in all activities.
reference: Position demonstrations for clear sight lines from seated position. Ensure all physical activities have a seated equivalent. Wheelchair users lead the experiment record-keeping role. Universal design applied throughout.

prompt: A Grade 10 chemistry lesson on organic chemistry assumes students have studied functional groups in Grade 9. Adapt for a class that missed that content due to school closures.
reference: Provide a 10-minute recap lesson within the lesson. Functional group reference card stays on desk throughout. Pair students with stronger background with those who missed content. Prerequisite knowledge embedded.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 5 maths lesson on telling time for students who use 12-hour time in daily life but the curriculum requires 24-hour time. Bridge both systems.
reference: Begin with 12-hour time students know. Introduce 24-hour time as extension: 13:00 = 1pm. Conversion chart reference tool. Real-world contexts: bus timetables, flight schedules. Both systems practised equally.

prompt: Adapt a Grade 9 English lesson on debate for students in a culture where direct public disagreement is considered disrespectful. Same critical thinking and argumentation objectives.
reference: Frame as 'exploring multiple perspectives' not confrontational debate. Use structured academic controversy format where students argue both sides. Written argument before oral. Teacher models respectful disagreement explicitly.
